title: Support for `unused-for` filter for pruning
body:
### Feature request description

We run podman builds in CI, and those builds share a build cache to speed things up. Managing this build cache becomes tricky because "poorly layering" containerfiles can quickly swamp the cache, and our only facilities to prune are by `until` which is the "creation" date, not "last used" date https://docs.podman.io/en/stable/markdown/podman-system-prune.1.html#filter-filters. Typically the "most used" layers are going to be old (since they rarely change), so we end up pruning the layers we are most interested in keeping.

The docker daemon has configuration for GC that can clean up layers that haven't been used in X amount of time https://docs.docker.com/build/cache/garbage-collection/#docker-daemon-configuration-file that Im taking some inspiration from.

comments.body:
@seanlafferty-ibm, could you help add a new filter option, after?

This sounds like an interesting idea to have some command for this type of GC. I think a big part of this should be implemented in containers-libs/storage. WDYT @mtrmac?

I don’t think we are currently _tracking_ a “last used” date; certainly we do not track it for _all_ layers/images, incl. those used by `podman run`.

Maybe we do (or easily could/should) track this for build-produced cache objects? Cc: @flouthoc .

Tracking this would be _some_ performance cost (\*shrug\*). For images, it looks plausible; for layers, it would add more operations to a path with a known locking bug for the non-default mount-based graph drivers (btrfs, zfs), that worries me a bit.
In buildah we already have operations where we filter intermediate images using timestamp for example `--cache-ttl` , I think it shouldn't be too hard to add something similar to `prune`.
@seanlafferty-ibm What are your thoughts on the new option for `podman system prune`? Do you think it would help solve your issue?

I think `--cache-ttl` solves a different issue. It will prevent reusing a layer if it was _created_ more than `X` ago. The use case I was hoping to address is to prune layers that haven't been _used_ recently. This way I can reclaim space without loosing the most re-used (typically oldest) layers.

For instance, we have 30 different containers all built off a similar ubi:9 base. We want those layers hot, but as the day goes on poorly-layered builds can eat up space, triggering a prune. Given our base layers are being hit almost every 5mins, I would like to keep those, and eject things that haven't been touched in hours.

@seanlafferty-ibm Yes my bad if you got confused with my comment, I was just trying to say we do have some flags in buildah where is filter intermediate images on the basis of timestamp so we can explore similar behaviour in other commands too, definitely `--cache-ttl` has nothing to do with this feature request.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I like this idea. Basically some sort of lru, which could pair nicely with an [RFE](https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/26191) I had a while back for auto reclaiming storage based on user configuration.
Hello, I am a UT Austin student interested on working on this issue. Could you please assign it to me?

A couple of quick questions before I begin implementing this, how should we handle cases where images/layers do not have a "last recently used" date where new images/layers being added to the cache will have this data field? Basically, what should we do with the images/layers that haven't been used since this feature was added, should they show up when looking for something older than 30 days for example? Another question, do we want to care about just images or also layers? And if we do layers, do we want to recursively go through each layer down the chain as its parent is used to update the last recently used time?
Hi, I don't think I will have enough time to finish this issue. Good luck to anyone else who takes this up in the future.

Being able to filter for and remove container images by their last use date would greatly simplify the cleanup when using of podman within CI. For a specific example see GitLab Runner that supports running its jobs with podman. They do not provide any ability to clean up container images, so they start piling up. Thus, a systemd timer or cron job is needed that prunes periodically. Since no differentiation between often or rarely used images is possible this may end up removing commonly used images which then have to be downloaded again, instead of keeping those around and only removing the rarely used ones. This increases build times, because downloading the container images at the start of the job takes time, and adds additional costs for data transfer when using cloud hosting.
Want to do an upvote here. For Docker usecases we tested the LRU based cleanup using https://github.com/stepchowfun/docuum - but seems like the project doesn't want to support Podman.
